metadata.title: Ship It: Export, Name, Submit + the Jargon Test
metadata.playlist: Sell the Vision — Your Investor Pitch Video
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 7 brief — Video Pitch to Investors: pre-submit checklist, export, naming, Canvas submit

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 7 of 7. You have a pitch. You have a recording. Now the last gates before you submit. Most students skip these. They are tired at the end of the project, they export the file, drop it into Canvas, and move on. Then they get the grade back and find out they lost points on jargon, on a running time that was 8 minutes and 22 seconds, on a filename the grader could not find. This episode is the checklist you run before you submit. It is short because each item is a yes or no. The jargon test, the timing gate, the file naming convention, and the two-file submit. None of these take more than 15 minutes to check. All of them can cost you points if you skip them.

props.segment: Ship It: Export, Name, Submit + the Jargon Test
props.command: Play my pitch for someone who has never heard of the project. Every place they look confused is a jargon fix before I submit.
props.runningText: seven gates before submit — jargon test, timing, file naming, two-file Canvas submit. Each is a yes or no…
output: Timing: under 8 min confirmed · Jargon test: real non-technical person, no confused looks
Canvas: both files submitted · no submission = no grade

beats.narration_text: The pre-submission checklist. Seven items. Run through each before you export. One: time is under 8 minutes. Not 8:05. Under 8:00. Record the final take and check the timestamp before exporting. Two: zero jargon, tested on a real non-technical person — not a teammate. A family member, a roommate, a friend outside the class. Every moment they look confused is a rewrite. Three: a visual on screen for every slide. If any slide has no image, no chart, no diagram — add one or use a text-heavy slide with large font. Four: problem clear in minute one. Play the first 60 seconds. If a non-technical person could not state the problem after that 60 seconds, the opening is not done. Five: business model present. If you did not record slide 5, you are missing points. Six: specific ask stated. If the last thing you say is 'that's my pitch, thank you,' you left the ask on the table. Seven: face on camera at some point. Check the recording — does your face appear? Any one of these fails costs you points that a good pitch earns back.

artifactLines: 1. Under 8:00. Not 8:05. Check the timestamp on the final take.
2. Zero jargon — tested on a real non-technical person (not a teammate). Every confused look = rewrite.
3. A visual on screen for every slide.
4. Problem clear in minute one. Play first 60 sec — can a non-technical person state the problem?
5. Business model present (slide 5). 6. Specific ask stated (slide 10). 7. Face on camera.
Any one of these fails costs points a good pitch earns back.
props.sparkLine: Seven yes/no gates. Each one is worth points.

beats.narration_text: Export and naming. You submit two files. The video file: MP4 or MOV format, or a Zoom or Loom share link if your school accepts links. The PDF file: export your slides as PDF from Google Slides, PowerPoint, or Keynote. Both files have the same naming convention: Your last name, underscore, your first name, underscore, 'MadisonPitch', then the extension. Smith underscore John underscore MadisonPitch.mp4 and Smith underscore John underscore MadisonPitch.pdf. No spaces. Underscores, not hyphens. Your actual name, not a project title. The grader reads dozens of submissions. Wrong filename means the grader has to hunt for your file, and that friction starts the review with a bad impression before the video plays. Check the filename before you upload. After uploading to Canvas: confirm both files show as attached. Refresh the submission page. Confirm again. 'No submission equals no grade' is in the rubric — and 'I submitted it but Canvas lost it' is not an appeal the grader can act on.

artifactLines: Video: MP4 or MOV, or Zoom/Loom share link. PDF: export slides as PDF.
Filename: LastName_FirstName_MadisonPitch.mp4 and .pdf — exact format.
No spaces. Underscores, not hyphens. Your name, not the project title.
After uploading: refresh Canvas and confirm both files show as attached.
'No submission = no grade' is in the rubric. Confirm the upload before logging off.
props.sparkLine: Wrong filename = grader hunts for your file. That's a bad start before the video plays.

beats.act: the jargon test — the final read-aloud
beats.narration_text: The jargon test is the most important pre-submit step and the one most students skip. Here is how it works. Find someone who has never heard of your project — a family member, a roommate, a friend who is not in the class. Play your pitch for them. Watch their face, not the screen. Every moment they look confused, raise an eyebrow, or glance away is a jargon signal. After the pitch, ask them two questions: what does the tool do, and who is it for? If they cannot answer those two questions clearly, the opening is not landing. Ask them to name one term they did not understand. That is your top priority rewrite before the final take. The jargon test is the same discipline as the Conductor Brief Part 1 read-aloud from the first series — if someone who has never heard of your project does not get it in the first hearing, the sentence needs rewriting. The pitch version of that test is: every confused look is a rewrite before you submit.

props.artifactHeading: Play it for someone who has never heard of the project
artifactLines: Find a non-technical person outside the class — family, roommate, friend.
Play the pitch. Watch their face, not the screen. Confused look = jargon signal.
After: ask 'what does the tool do?' and 'who is it for?' — if they can't answer, rewrite the opening.
Ask: 'name one term you didn't understand.' That's your top-priority fix before the final take.
Same rule as the Conductor Brief Part 1 read-aloud: every confused look is a rewrite.
props.sparkLine: Watch their face. Every confused look is a rewrite before you submit.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for the series. Seven episodes. Ten slides. One pitch video. The whole arc in one pass. Episode 1: 10/20/30, 80/20, who the audience is. Episodes 2 and 3: title, problem, solution, magic — in plain English, with quantified pain and a seventh-grader-level one-liner. Episodes 4 and 5: business model, go-to-market, competition, ask, and proof of concept at whatever stage you are at. Episode 6: record it, time it, hit under 8 minutes. This episode: run the checklist, run the jargon test, name the files correctly, confirm both uploaded. The series does not cover marketing, growth, investor relations, or fundraising — it covers the 10-slide pitch that introduces all of those conversations. Nail the 10 slides, stay under 8 minutes, translate every technical term, make a specific ask. That is the pitch that gets a follow-up conversation.

props.artifactTitle: Ship It: Export, Name, Submit + the Jargon Test
artifactLines: Pre-submit checklist: under 8 min · zero jargon (tested) · visual every slide · problem clear min 1 · biz model · ask · face.
Files: LastName_FirstName_MadisonPitch.mp4 + .pdf — exact naming, both uploaded to Canvas.
Jargon test: non-technical person, watch their face, every confused look = rewrite.
10 slides, 8 min, 30-point font. 80/20: all slides clean and jargon-free first, then memorable.
The pitch introduces the business conversation — it doesn't close a deal.
Nail the 10 slides, stay under 8 min, translate everything, make a specific ask.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. One final prompt — the jargon check. Paste this into Claude with your final pitch script before you record the last take. The prompt reads: Here is my complete pitch script — all 10 slides: [paste the full script]. Run the jargon-audit pass. Flag every technical term, abbreviation, acronym, or phrase that a non-technical investor might not immediately understand. For each flagged term, suggest a plain-English replacement. Then read my slide 1 one-liner and slide 2 problem statement. If a non-technical person heard only those two slides, would they know what the tool does, who it is for, and why it matters? If not, suggest one specific revision for each. After the prompt runs: fix every jargon flag. Then run the real test — play the pitch for a non-technical person before recording the final take. The Claude jargon audit is the machine check. The human jargon test is the one that tells you if it actually lands. Both checks, in that order, before the final recording.

props.segment: Ship It: Export, Name, Submit + the Jargon Test
props.command: Here is my complete pitch script — all 10 slides: [paste full script]. Run the jargon-audit pass: flag every technical term, abbreviation, or phrase a non-technical investor wouldn't immediately understand. Suggest plain-English replacements. Then check: if a non-technical person heard only slides 1–2, would they know what the tool does, who it's for, and why it matters? If not, suggest one specific revision for each.
props.runningText: paste your full pitch script before running — then take the machine audit to a real human test before the final recording…

beats.narration_text: Ship It: Export, Name, Submit + the Jargon Test. Liam, in for Bear.
